revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Suō Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[category:Suō Province]] [[category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] to [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]]

This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]]

When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamanooya Jinja
玉祖神社 | image = Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), haiden.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamanooya Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Yamaguchi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|3|27.75|N|131|32|1.15|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamanooya-no-Mikoto; Ishikori-dome no Mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1690 Ōaza Ōsaki, Hōfu-shi, Yamaguchi-ken 747-0065 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamanooya Jinja'''|玉祖神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ōsaki neighborhood of the city of [[Hōfu, Yamaguchi|Hōfu]] in [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Suō Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the Saturday & Sunday nearest September 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamanooya Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Tamanooya-no-Mikoto]]'''| 玉祖命|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and the creator of the ''[[magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|Yasakani no Magatama]]''. * {{nihongo|'''Ishikori-dome no Mikoto'''| 石凝姥命|}}, the goddess of mirrors and stonecutters, and creator of the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. ==History== The origins of Tamanooya Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for a clan of [[Kofun period]] craftsmen who made ''magatama'' and tubular beads, who regarded Tamanooya-no-Mikoto as their ancestor. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamanooya-jinja}} *[https://visit-hofu.jp/spots/tamanooya-shrine Hofu Tourist information] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Suō Province]] [[Category:Hōfu, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]][[en:Tamanooya Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Cqqqqqqqqq1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Cqqqqqqqqq1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to cqqqqqqqqq5]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to cqqqqqqqqq5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamanooya Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', when the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]] led an expedition to conquer the [[Kumaso]], he was created by a clan headed by Princess Kamikashi, who offered him sacred treasures, and he prayed at the Tamanooya Jinja for victory. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the Kamakura period, the monk [[Chōgen (monk)|Chōgen]] who visited the Saba River basin to procure materials for the reconstruction of reconstruction of [[Tōdai-ji]] and from March 1186 the province donated substantial portion of its revenues to the project. When the reconstruction was completed in 1195, Chōgen rebuilt the Tamanooya Jinja as a gesture of thanksgiving, and the shrine has preserved the {{nihongo|'''List of Treasures of the Suō Province Ichinomiya Reconstruction'''|周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録| Suō koku ichinomiya Zōtai shinden takaramono-tō mokuroku}} written by Chōgen listing the work accomplished. This document is noteworthy as the most credible example of Chōgen's handwriting and is a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/9001|title=周防国一宮造替神殿宝物等目録〈建久六年九月廿八日／重源加判〉|trans-title=List of treasures, etc. of the Suō Kuni Ichinomiya Reconstruction Temple|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 45-minute walk from [[Hōfu Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Sanyō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), honden.jpg|Honden File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), shinmon.jpg|Gate File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), ichinotorii-1.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamanooya-jinja (Hofu), Tamanoiwaya.jpg|Tama-no-iwaya ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe.
